walkthrough.title: Choose a Map and Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Welcome, brave explorer! The Queen has called upon the Guild, and your first expedition awaits. Begin by giving each player a matching map board. For your maiden voyage, we recommend the Avenia map -- it features the classic terrain types without any special rules. Then hand each player a matching set of 13 village tokens and 36 explorer cubes in their chosen color. Return any unused maps and token sets to the box.

walkthrough.title: Place the Exploration Board
walkthrough.text: Set the Exploration Board in the center of the table where all players can reach it. This board serves as your expedition command center -- it has labeled slots for each era card and a space for the Exploration Deck. Place the Era II, Era III, and Era I/II/III cards on their matching spots on the board. These cards will be shuffled into the deck as you progress through the four eras of the game.

walkthrough.title: Build the Era I Exploration Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the Era I card and the 5 basic explore cards. Shuffle these 6 cards together to form the Exploration Deck for the first era. Place this deck face down beside the Exploration Board. This deck drives the entire game -- each turn, you will reveal one card and all players simultaneously take the explore action shown on it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Investigate Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 28 investigate cards together and place them face down beside the Exploration Board. These cards grant powerful special explore actions that you will draft throughout the game. When era cards are revealed, each player draws 2 investigate cards and keeps 1, building a personal toolkit of advanced exploration abilities.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Treasure Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 40 treasure cards and place them face down near the Exploration Board. Ancient ruins scattered across the map hold these treasures. Some grant immediate bonuses like extra coins or a free explorer placement, while others score points at the end of the game based on your villages, discovery towers, or collected sets of vases.

walkthrough.title: Stock the Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the 16 discovery towers, 32 trading post tokens, 40 treasure tokens, and all the coin tokens in a shared supply within reach of every player. You will claim items from this supply throughout the game as you explore discovery spaces, complete trade routes, uncover ruins, and visit coin spaces on your map.

walkthrough.title: Deal Goal Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the 6 goal cards that match the map you are using (each map has its own set). Shuffle these 6 cards, then deal 3 face up below the Exploration Board. Return the remaining 3 to the box unseen. These goals provide bonus coins for achieving specific map objectives -- the first player to complete a goal earns the higher reward, while later completions earn the lower amount.

walkthrough.title: Understand Your Map
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to survey your map before the expedition begins. Locate your capital space -- this is your starting point for exploration in every era. Identify the four terrain types: grassland, desert, mountain, and sea. Notice the cities with numbered values, the ruin spaces, the discovery spaces, and the coin spaces. The score area at the bottom of your map shows how much villages and discovery towers are worth based on the era. You are now ready to chart the kingdom of Tigom!

summary.title: Understand Your Map
bullets: Capital space = your starting exploration point each era
4 terrain types: grassland, desert, mountain, sea
Key spaces: cities (numbered), ruins, discovery, coins
Score area at bottom shows village and discovery tower values per era

walkthrough.title: Game Structure: Four Eras of Exploration
walkthrough.text: The Guild of Merchant Explorers unfolds over 4 eras, each consisting of several turns. During each era, you will flip cards from the Exploration Deck one at a time. Every card reveals an explore action that all players perform simultaneously on their own maps. When the deck runs out, the era ends. With each new era, the deck grows larger as new era cards are shuffled in, giving you more turns and more powerful actions as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Taking an Explore Action
walkthrough.text: When an explore card is revealed, all players place explorer cubes onto their maps according to the card's instructions. The 5 basic explore cards let you place explorers on specific terrain types: 2 grassland spaces, 2 desert spaces, 1 mountain space, 3 sea spaces in a straight line, or 2 adjacent spaces of any terrain. Each explorer must be placed adjacent to your capital space, a village you have discovered, or a previously placed explorer cube. You always place explorers one at a time.

walkthrough.title: The Golden Rules of Exploration
walkthrough.text: Five iron-clad rules govern every explore action and can never be broken. First, each explorer must be placed adjacent to one of your villages or one of your existing explorers, on a space without any piece. Second, always place explorers one at a time. Third, resolve map features as soon as their requirements are met, even mid-action. Fourth, unless a card specifically requires a connected group, you may place explorers anywhere on your map as long as each one is adjacent to a village or explorer. Fifth, you may always place fewer explorers than a card allows, but you cannot save unused placements for later.

walkthrough.title: Discovering Villages by Completing Regions
walkthrough.text: A region is a contiguous group of same-terrain spaces. When you place explorers on every space within a single region during one era, you must immediately replace one explorer on a blank space with a village token from your supply. This village is permanent -- it stays on your map for the rest of the game, serving as a new starting point for future exploration. You earn coins equal to the village value shown in the score area for the current era. Each region can only ever have one village.

walkthrough.title: Connecting Cities and Completing Trade Routes
walkthrough.text: City spaces have numbered values (2, 3, 4, or 5). When you create an unbroken chain of explorers, villages, and/or your capital connecting two cities that do not already have trading posts between them, you complete a trade route. Multiply the two city values together and earn that many coins immediately. Then place a trading post on one of the two cities. A city with a trading post can never score another trade route, so plan your connections carefully.

walkthrough.title: Exploring Discovery Spaces
walkthrough.text: Discovery spaces are wild terrain -- you can explore them with any explore action. When you place an explorer on a discovery space, immediately return that explorer to your supply and place a discovery tower in its place. Earn coins equal to the value shown in the score area based on how many discovery towers you have placed total. Discovery towers are permanent and block the space from future exploration, so each discovery space can only be claimed once across the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Uncovering Ruins and Drawing Treasure
walkthrough.text: When you explore a ruin space for the first time, place a treasure token under your explorer on that space and immediately draw a treasure card. Some treasure cards have immediate effects -- bonus coins or a free explorer placement -- which you resolve right away before discarding the card face down to the bottom of the deck. Other treasure cards score coins at end of game based on your villages, discovery towers, or collected vase sets, and you keep these face down beside your map until final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Earning Coins from Coin Spaces
walkthrough.text: Some map spaces show coin icons. When you place an explorer on a coin space, immediately take that many coins from the supply. Unlike ruins, coin spaces can be explored again in later eras to earn their coins again. These recurring payoffs make coin spaces near your capital especially valuable since you can reach them repeatedly across multiple eras.

walkthrough.title: Era Cards and Investigate Cards
walkthrough.text: Era cards are special explore cards shuffled into the deck at the start of each era. When an era card is revealed for the first time, each player draws 2 investigate cards and keeps 1, placing it beside their map in the slot matching that era. Perform the investigate card's explore action immediately. In later eras, when that same era card reappears, you use the investigate card you previously drafted for it. In Era IV, the I/II/III era card lets you reuse any one of your three investigate cards, effectively doubling one of your best actions.

walkthrough.title: End of an Era: Reset and Rebuild the Deck
walkthrough.text: When the Exploration Deck runs out, the era ends. Remove all explorer cubes from your map and return them to your personal supply. Villages, trading posts, treasure tokens, and discovery towers remain permanently. Then build the next era's Exploration Deck: Era II uses the 5 basic cards plus Era I and Era II cards (7 total). Era III adds Era III (8 total). Era IV uses all 9 cards. More cards mean more turns and more opportunities each era.

walkthrough.title: Completing Goals for Bonus Coins
walkthrough.text: Three goal cards are on display for the entire game. As soon as you meet a goal's requirements during an explore action, place an explorer from your supply on that goal card. If you are the first player to complete it, you earn the higher coin reward shown on the card. All subsequent completions earn the lower amount. If multiple players complete the same goal on the same turn, they all earn the higher amount. Once you complete a goal, you cannot complete it again.

walkthrough.title: End of the Game and Determining the Winner
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the final turn of Era IV. Reveal any face-down treasure cards that score at end of game and add those coins to your total. The player with the most coins wins the game and earns the Queen's highest honors. In case of a tie, the player with the most discovery towers wins. If still tied, the player with the most villages wins. If the tie persists, the explorers share the glory of victory together.

walkthrough.title: Start Your First Expedition
walkthrough.text: For this practice game, we recommend 2-3 players using the Avenia map. Avenia has no special rules and features a nice variety of terrain, cities, and ruins. Set up the game as you learned: each player gets a map, cubes, and village tokens. Build the Era I deck with the 5 basic cards and the Era I card. Lay out the 3 goal cards and stock the supply. Place the Investigate and Treasure Decks nearby. Survey your map and find your capital space in the center of the island.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the First Card
walkthrough.text: One player flips the top card of the Exploration Deck. Suppose it is Explore 2 Grassland Spaces. Everyone now looks at their map for grassland spaces adjacent to their capital. Remember, you place explorers one at a time, and the two grassland spaces do not need to be adjacent to each other -- they just each need to be next to your capital, a village, or a previously placed explorer. Place your first cube, then your second.

walkthrough.title: Think About Region Completion
walkthrough.text: As you place those grassland explorers, look at the grassland regions near your capital. If a region only has 2 grassland spaces and you just covered both, congratulations -- you have completed a region! Immediately replace one of those explorers on a blank space with a village token. Check the score area on your map for Era I village value and take that many coins from the supply. This new village is now a permanent launchpad for future exploration.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Next Card: Desert Exploration
walkthrough.text: Flip the next card. If it is Explore 2 Desert Spaces, look for desert terrain adjacent to your capital, your new village, or any explorer already on your map. The two desert spaces can be placed in completely different parts of your map if you have villages in multiple locations. In Era I you likely only have your capital and perhaps one village, so your options may be limited -- that is fine, you are just getting started.

walkthrough.title: The Mountain Card: Quality Over Quantity
walkthrough.text: When Explore 1 Mountain Space comes up, you only place a single explorer, but mountain spaces are often strategically important. Mountains can bridge gaps between regions, connect you toward distant cities, or reach discovery spaces. Place your mountain explorer thoughtfully -- even a single cube in the right spot can open up a world of possibilities on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Navigating the Seas
walkthrough.text: The sea card -- Explore 3 Sea Spaces in a Straight Line -- is unique because all 3 explorers must form a straight line, and they must be adjacent to each other in sequence. The first explorer goes next to a village or explorer, the second next to the first, and the third next to either the first or second, forming a line. This is powerful for crossing water to reach islands, but the straight-line requirement means you need to plan your angle carefully.

walkthrough.title: The Versatile Any-Terrain Card
walkthrough.text: Explore Any 2 Adjacent Spaces is the most flexible basic card. Both explorers can go on any terrain, but they must be adjacent to each other. The first must be adjacent to a village or explorer, and the second must be adjacent to the first. This card is fantastic for connecting different terrain types, bridging toward a city, or finishing off a small region regardless of terrain.

walkthrough.title: Your First Era Card and Investigate Draft
walkthrough.text: When the Era I card is revealed, something special happens. Each player draws 2 investigate cards from the Investigate Deck and chooses 1 to keep, placing it in the Era I slot beside your map. Discard the other face down to the bottom of the deck. Now perform the explore action on the investigate card you chose. These investigate actions are often more powerful than basic cards -- perhaps exploring up to 4 sea spaces, or placing explorers in a continuous path across 2 terrain types.

walkthrough.title: End of Era I: The Great Reset
walkthrough.text: After all 6 cards have been revealed and resolved, Era I ends. Now comes a key moment: remove every explorer cube from your map and return them to your supply. Only permanent pieces remain -- villages, trading posts, treasure tokens, and discovery towers. Your map may look sparse again, but those villages you discovered are now launching points scattered across the map. Build the Era II deck by shuffling the 5 basic cards plus Era I and Era II cards (7 cards total).

walkthrough.title: Era II: Expanding Your Network
walkthrough.text: Era II gives you 7 turns instead of 6, and you now have villages to explore from in addition to your capital. This dramatically expands your reach. Focus on completing new regions to discover more villages, which will further multiply your starting positions in Era III. Keep an eye on the goal cards -- some goals involve exploring specific locations or discovering villages in particular areas.

walkthrough.title: Completing Your First Trade Route
walkthrough.text: Let's say you have been building a chain of explorers from one city toward another. When your explorer chain, combined with any villages and your capital, forms an unbroken connection between two cities without trading posts, you complete a trade route! Multiply the city values together. For example, connecting a 3-city to a 4-city earns you 12 coins. Place a trading post on one of those cities. Choose wisely -- a city with a trading post cannot score another trade route.

walkthrough.title: Visiting a Discovery Space
walkthrough.text: Discovery spaces are wild, meaning any explore card can place an explorer on them. When you land on a discovery space, immediately swap your explorer for a discovery tower from the supply. Check the score area on your map -- your first tower might be worth 2 coins, your second worth 5, your third worth 9, and so on. The escalating value makes discovery towers increasingly rewarding. Plan your path to hit as many as possible across the game.

walkthrough.title: Exploring Your First Ruin
walkthrough.text: When your explorer reaches a ruin space for the first time, place a treasure token under the explorer and draw a treasure card. Read the card carefully. If it says something like 'Immediately place 1 explorer on any adjacent space' or 'Immediately earn 2 coins,' resolve that effect right now. If the card shows end-game scoring like '1 coin per village on grassland,' keep it face down by your map. You will score it after Era IV.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting to Resolve Features Immediately
walkthrough.text: One of the trickiest rules for new explorers is the third Golden Rule: resolve map features as soon as their requirements are met, even if you have not finished placing all your explorers for a card. If your first explorer completes a region, you must discover a village and score it before placing your second explorer. This matters because that new village could then serve as a starting point for your remaining explorers on the same turn!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Trying to Score a City with a Trading Post Again
walkthrough.text: Once a trading post sits on a city, that city is locked out of future trade routes. New players sometimes forget this and try to connect a traded city to a third city for another big payout. It will not work. When placing your trading post, think about which city has fewer useful connections remaining and place the post there, preserving the other city for a future, potentially more lucrative route.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring the Sea Straight-Line Rule
walkthrough.text: The sea card requires 3 explorers in a straight line, and new players often try to place them in an L-shape or curve. All three must be in a straight line on the hex grid. Before placing, trace a straight path across the sea spaces to verify your intended placement forms a valid line. If you cannot form a straight line of 3, you are allowed to place fewer -- even just 1 sea explorer is legal.

walkthrough.title: Era III: The Tempo Quickens
walkthrough.text: Era III adds another era card for 8 total turns. You now draft a third investigate card, giving you a potent arsenal of special actions. With villages spread across the map, you can reach far-flung corners of the kingdom. This is a great era to complete trade routes between distant cities and push toward discovery spaces you could not reach before. Village values also increase in later eras, so completing regions now is even more rewarding.

walkthrough.title: Era IV: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: The grand finale! Era IV uses all 9 cards, giving you the most turns. Crucially, you do not draft a new investigate card. Instead, when the I/II/III era card is revealed, you choose any one of your three investigate cards to use again. Pick your most powerful action for this decisive double play. This is your last chance to complete goals, finish trade routes, and reach the remaining discovery spaces.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring: Tally Your Treasure
walkthrough.text: After Era IV, the expedition concludes. Reveal any face-down treasure cards and add their end-game coin values to your total. Vase treasure cards score as a set: a full set of 4 different vases is worth 16 coins, and each additional vase beyond that scores 4 coins. Count up all your coins from trade routes, villages, discovery towers, coin spaces, goals, and treasures. The wealthiest explorer wins!

walkthrough.title: Reviewing the Tiebreakers
walkthrough.text: If two players end with the same coin total, the player with the most discovery towers wins. If still tied, the player with the most villages wins. And if the tie persists, those explorers share the victory together -- a fitting end for adventurers who charted the kingdom equally well.

walkthrough.title: Reflect on Your Journey
walkthrough.text: After your first game, take a moment to examine what worked and what did not. Did you discover enough villages early to have good starting positions in later eras? Did you prioritize trade routes between high-value cities? Did your investigate cards complement your strategy? Each game of Guild plays out differently because the card order changes, the investigate cards you draft vary, and the goal cards demand different approaches. Every expedition teaches you something new about the kingdom of Tigom.

walkthrough.title: Ready for New Horizons
walkthrough.text: Now that you have conquered Avenia, consider trying a new map. Aghon adds lettered ruin spaces and island geography. Kazan introduces volcanoes you cannot cross and mountain regions without blank spaces. Cnidaria features sonulux crystal spaces that accumulate coins and jagged reefs that block your path. Each map is a fresh adventure with unique challenges and goal cards. The Queen's kingdom awaits your next expedition -- chart on, brave explorer!

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Early Villages
walkthrough.text: Villages are the lifeblood of your expedition. The more villages you discover in Eras I and II, the more starting points you have in later eras, letting you reach distant corners of the map. Target small regions first -- a 2-space grassland region can be completed in a single turn, instantly giving you a permanent foothold in that area. Think of villages as expedition base camps that multiply your reach exponentially with each era.

walkthrough.title: Plan Trade Routes Around High-Value Cities
walkthrough.text: Trade routes score the product of two city values, so the difference between connecting a 2 and a 3 (6 coins) versus a 4 and a 5 (20 coins) is enormous. Identify the highest-value city pairs on your map early and start building toward those connections. Sometimes it is worth spending an entire era bridging toward a lucrative pair rather than grabbing smaller payoffs elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Draft Investigate Cards That Fit Your Map Position
walkthrough.text: When you draw 2 investigate cards, choose the one that complements where your villages already are and what you plan to do next era. A card that lets you explore 4 sea spaces is incredible if you need to cross water to reach a high-value city. A card that explores a continuous path of 2 terrain types is perfect for completing a region that spans mixed terrain. Do not just pick the card that looks powerful in isolation -- pick the one that fits your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Keep an Eye on Goal Card Timing
walkthrough.text: The first player to complete a goal earns significantly more coins than subsequent completers. Glance at the goals during setup and identify which ones align with your natural exploration paths. If a goal asks you to discover a village adjacent to a discovery tower, plan to reach a discovery space near a completable region. Racing for first completion can be worth 5-10 extra coins, which often decides the game.

walkthrough.title: Use the Any-Terrain Card Strategically
walkthrough.text: The Explore Any 2 Adjacent Spaces card is the Swiss army knife of the deck. Save your mental planning for this card's arrival -- it is often the turn where you can finish a region, reach a ruin, or bridge the last gap to complete a trade route. Since you cannot predict exact card order, always have a flexible backup plan for when this versatile card appears.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect Discovery Towers
walkthrough.text: Discovery tower scoring escalates rapidly. Your first tower might net you only 2 coins, but your third could be worth 9. Over the course of the game, a player who places 3-4 discovery towers can earn 20+ coins from them alone. Since discovery spaces accept any terrain type, they can often be reached opportunistically when the right card appears. Weave discovery space visits into your broader exploration plans.

walkthrough.title: Revisit Coin Spaces Across Eras
walkthrough.text: Unlike most map features that only reward you once, coin spaces pay out every time you explore them. If a coin space is near your capital or a well-positioned village, you can harvest it every era for a reliable income stream. A 2-coin space explored 4 times across the game is 8 coins total -- not flashy, but consistent and guaranteed.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Other Players Are Doing
walkthrough.text: Even though everyone plays on their own map, the game has interactive elements through goals and investigate card drafting. If you see an opponent closing in on a goal you are also pursuing, you may need to accelerate your plans or pivot to a different goal. Similarly, since all players reveal the same explore cards, pay attention to which regions and routes others are building -- it helps you gauge when goals will be contested.

summary.title: Prioritize Early Villages
bullets: Complete small regions in Eras I-II for permanent starting points
More villages = exponentially more reach in later eras

summary.title: Plan Trade Routes Around High-Value Cities
bullets: Multiply city values: 4 x 5 = 20 coins vs. 2 x 3 = 6 coins
Start building toward high-value city pairs early

summary.title: Draft Investigate Cards That Fit Your Map Position
bullets: Choose cards that complement your village positions and strategy
A powerful card in the wrong spot is worse than a modest card in the right spot

summary.title: Keep an Eye on Goal Card Timing
bullets: First completion earns significantly more coins
Align your exploration with the goals on display

summary.title: Use the Any-Terrain Card Strategically
bullets: Most flexible basic card; plan key moves around it
Ideal for finishing regions, reaching ruins, or bridging trade routes

summary.title: Do Not Neglect Discovery Towers
bullets: Escalating value: 3-4 towers can yield 20+ coins total
Wild terrain means any explore card can reach them

summary.title: Revisit Coin Spaces Across Eras
bullets: Coin spaces pay every era when re-explored
Coin spaces near capital or villages = reliable income

summary.title: Watch What Other Players Are Doing
bullets: Goals are contested: accelerate or pivot based on opponents
Same cards for everyone; observe others' progress on goals

walkthrough.title: The Village Snowball: Maximizing Era I Regions
walkthrough.text: Elite explorers know that Era I village placement dictates the entire game. On Avenia, identify the 2-space regions near your capital and aim to complete at least 2 regions in the first era. Each village discovered in Era I gives you 4 eras of utility as a starting point. Map out which basic cards you need for each target region and have contingency plans for card order. A player who discovers 3 villages in Era I versus 1 has a nearly insurmountable positional advantage heading into Era II.

walkthrough.title: Trade Route Multiplication: Targeting the 5x4 and 5x5 Jackpots
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring trade routes in the game connect 5-value cities. A 5x5 connection yields 25 coins from a single trade route -- nearly a quarter of a competitive winning score. Even 5x4 (20 coins) and 5x3 (15 coins) are game-defining. Advanced players identify these premium routes during setup and orient their entire village placement strategy around creating a path between them. Sometimes it is optimal to skip a small region village to instead position toward a major trade route.

walkthrough.title: Investigate Card Synergy Chains
walkthrough.text: Your three investigate cards form a toolkit you will use across Eras II, III, and IV (with one used twice in Era IV). Think of them as a cohesive system rather than individual picks. If your Era I card gives you strong sea coverage, look for an Era II card that covers mountain or desert -- terrain your basic cards may struggle with. The Era III pick should fill whatever gap remains. In Era IV, plan your I/II/III card reuse around whichever investigate action has the highest impact given your end-game board state.

walkthrough.title: Discovery Tower Acceleration
walkthrough.text: The escalating discovery tower scoring curve heavily rewards collecting them quickly. If you can place your third tower in Era II instead of Era III, you earn the high-value payout one era earlier and have more turns in subsequent eras to reach the fourth tower. Position villages near discovery spaces specifically so you can reach them with any terrain card. Since discovery spaces are wild, they work with every basic explore card -- the challenge is purely one of adjacency.

walkthrough.title: Treasure Card Set Collection: The Vase Gambit
walkthrough.text: Vase treasure cards score 16 coins for a full set of 4 and 4 coins for each additional vase beyond that. This means a player who hits 3-4 ruin spaces and draws well can earn 16-20 coins from vases alone. Plan your exploration to pass through as many ruin spaces as possible, especially in earlier eras when the treasure deck is fresh. Even non-vase treasures scoring 1 coin per village or per discovery tower can be worth 4-6 coins in a strong game.

walkthrough.title: The Dual-Front Strategy
walkthrough.text: Rather than expanding in a single direction from your capital, advanced players often build two separate exploration fronts heading toward different parts of the map. By discovering villages on opposite sides of the map in Era I or II, you create two independent expansion zones. This lets you complete regions and trade routes on both fronts simultaneously, maximizing the value of every explore card regardless of terrain type.

walkthrough.title: Goal Card Sequencing and Denial
walkthrough.text: Study the three goal cards and estimate how many turns each requires. If one goal naturally falls along your route, pursue it immediately for the first-completion bonus. If you see opponents converging on a goal you are also close to, sometimes it is correct to divert one turn to complete it first, even if it slightly delays your other plans. The difference between the high and low reward on a goal card can be 4-8 coins -- equivalent to a small trade route.

walkthrough.title: Era IV End-Game Optimization
walkthrough.text: In Era IV, shift from long-term positioning to pure point extraction. Every explorer placement should earn immediate coins or complete a scoring feature. Revisit high-value coin spaces. Complete the last achievable trade routes. Reach remaining discovery spaces. Score any goals you have not yet completed. The I/II/III investigate card reuse is your wild card -- plan several turns in advance for exactly when and where to deploy your most powerful investigate action for maximum final-era impact.

walkthrough.title: Map-Specific Mastery: Adapting to Unique Terrain
walkthrough.text: Each map fundamentally changes optimal strategy. Cnidaria's crystal spaces create an accumulating bonus that rewards early and frequent visits -- treat them like investment accounts. Kazan's impassable volcanoes and village-free mountain regions force you to value sea routes and carefully plan around blockages. Aghon's island geography makes the sea straight-line card disproportionately powerful. Before the game begins, study your map's unique features and adjust your village placement priorities accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Coin Efficiency: Points Per Action Analysis
walkthrough.text: Top players think in terms of coins per explorer placed. A 2-space region yielding 3 coins for a village is 1.5 coins per explorer. A 5x4 trade route that required 6 explorers to connect yields 3.3 coins per explorer. A discovery tower reached with 1 explorer in Era III might yield 9 coins. Always evaluate whether your next placement earns enough relative to alternatives. In close games, the player who squeezed the most coins out of each explorer cube is the one who walks away with the Queen's favor.

entries.definition: One of the four basic terrain types. The basic explore card places up to 2 explorers on grassland spaces; they do not need to be adjacent to each other.
One of the four basic terrain types. The basic explore card places up to 2 explorers on desert spaces; they do not need to be adjacent to each other.
One of the four basic terrain types. The basic explore card places only 1 explorer on a mountain space. On the Kazan map, some mountain regions have no blank spaces and cannot contain villages.
One of the four basic terrain types. The basic explore card places up to 3 explorers on sea spaces in a straight line, with each placed adjacent to the previous one.
A contiguous group of spaces of the same terrain type. Completing all spaces in a region during one era discovers a village on a blank space within it. Each region can hold at most 1 village.

The starting space on each map, marked distinctly. You always explore from your capital as though it were a village, but it does not count as a village for goals. You cannot place an explorer on the capital space itself.

A terrain space with no special feature. When a region is completed, you must place your new village on a blank space within that region. Spaces with cities, coins, ruins, or discovery icons are not blank.
On the hex grid, a space is adjacent to each space that shares an edge with it (up to 6 neighbors). All explorer placements require adjacency to a village, the capital, or an existing explorer.
An area of terrain spaces isolated by sea, the border of the map, or both. Referenced by certain Avenia goal cards.

A permanent token placed on a blank space when a region is fully explored in a single era. Villages serve as starting points for future exploration and earn coins based on the era in which they are discovered.
Your main playing piece. Placed on map spaces during explore actions. All explorer cubes are removed from the map at the end of each era, returning to your personal supply.

A permanent piece placed on a discovery space when it is explored. Replaces the explorer cube. Earns escalating coin values based on total towers placed (see score area). The space cannot be explored again.
A token placed on one of two cities when a trade route is completed. The city with a trading post can never be used to score another trade route. Permanent once placed.

A marker placed under your explorer when you first explore a ruin space. Indicates that a treasure card has already been drawn for this ruin. Re-exploring the ruin in a later era does not grant another card.

The game's currency and victory points. Earned from trade routes, villages, discovery towers, coin spaces, goals, and treasure cards. Kept value-side down until end of game. The player with the most coins wins.

Placed on a sonulux crystal space when explored on the Cnidaria map. Earns coins equal to the total number of crystal spaces explored (cumulative). Remains permanently; re-exploring does not earn additional coins.
The face-down deck of explore cards that drives each era. Contains the 5 basic cards plus accumulated era cards. Grows from 6 cards in Era I to 9 cards in Era IV.

The 5 core explore cards used every era: Explore 2 Grassland, Explore 2 Desert, Explore 1 Mountain, Explore 3 Sea (straight line), and Explore 2 Any Adjacent. They form the foundation of every Exploration Deck.

Four special cards (I, II, III, and I/II/III) added to the Exploration Deck as the game progresses. When revealed for the first time, they trigger an investigate card draft. In subsequent eras, they activate your previously drafted investigate card.

28 unique cards with powerful special explore actions. Drafted when era cards appear for the first time (draw 2, keep 1). Each is slotted to a specific era. In Era IV, the I/II/III card lets you reuse any one of your three investigate cards.

40 cards drawn when exploring ruin spaces. Some have immediate effects (bonus coins, free explorer placement). Others score at end of game based on your villages, towers, or collected sets. Vase cards score as a set.

Map-specific objective cards. 3 are dealt each game from a pool of 6. First player to complete a goal earns the higher coin reward; subsequent completions earn the lower amount. Simultaneous completion awards the higher amount to all.
A map space showing coin icons. When explored, immediately earn the indicated number of coins. Unlike most features, coin spaces can be explored again in later eras for repeated payoff.

A map space (usually on sea terrain, but sometimes on other terrains) that grants a treasure card when first explored. Marked with a treasure token after first exploration. Re-exploring does not grant a second card.

A map space with a numbered value (2, 3, 4, or 5). When two cities are connected by an unbroken chain of explorers, villages, and/or capital, you complete a trade route and earn the product of their values in coins.

A wild terrain space that accepts any explore action. When explored, the explorer is replaced by a discovery tower. Earns escalating coins based on total towers placed. Cannot be explored again once a tower is placed.
A wild terrain space unique to the Cnidaria map. When explored, place a crystal token and earn coins equal to the total crystal spaces explored across all eras. Not part of any region.
An impassable space on the Kazan map. You cannot explore volcanoes or place explorers on them. They create natural barriers that shape your exploration routes.
An impassable sea feature on the Cnidaria map. You cannot explore spaces with jagged reefs. They block sea routes and force detours around them.
Completed when an unbroken chain connects two cities without trading posts. Score equals the product of the two city values. One city receives a trading post, locking it out of future routes.
When you discover a village, earn coins equal to the village value for the current era as shown in the score area. Later eras typically award more coins per village discovered.
Each discovery tower earns coins based on your total number of towers, shown in the score area. The value escalates: each successive tower is worth more than the previous one.
Vase treasure cards score as a set at end of game. A complete set of 4 different vases is worth 16 coins. Each additional vase card beyond the first set scores 4 coins.

One of the four phases of the game. Each era consists of turns equal to the number of cards in the Exploration Deck (6, 7, 8, then 9). At the end of each era, explorers are removed but permanent pieces remain.

Five inviolable rules: (1) place adjacent to village or explorer on empty spaces only, (2) place one at a time, (3) resolve features immediately, (4) non-connected placements allowed unless card requires it, (5) may place fewer but cannot save actions.
The recommended starter map. Features the capital city of Tigom, a large central island, and surrounding bays. Uses 'land' as a concept for certain goals. No additional special rules.
A map of small islands, blazing deserts, and mushroom forests. Cities perch on the ocean, making sea navigation vital. No additional special rules beyond its unique geography.
A volcanic map with impassable volcano spaces and mountain regions that may lack blank spaces (no villages possible). Ruin spaces are lettered for goal completion.
Home to sonulux crystal spaces (cumulative coin scoring) and jagged reefs (impassable). Crystal spaces are wild terrain but not part of any region. Features unique ruin symbols for goals.
